title: Green ash tool handle
subtitle: A drawknifed handle for a hammer or light mallet in green ash
excerpt: A replacement hammer or mallet handle from a green ash stave: 35 cm long, oval in cross-section, with a tapered eye end that wedges tight as the wood dries. Ash is the standard British handle wood; shock-resistant, straight-grained, and available from any hedgerow or coppiced woodland.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. I for handle dimensions and eye-fitting method; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for drawknife technique on ash.

content.text: Ash is the traditional British handle wood. It absorbs shock better than any common British hardwood. It splits along the grain in a way you can predict. And it grows in straight coppice rods that need little prep. A green ash handle is the first green-woodwork project for most beginners. The work is simple, the ways it can go wrong are few, and the result is useful straight away. The shaping is all drawknife work on a shaving horse: no lathe, no machinery.
The wood
A freshly cut ash stave: 38 cm long, 5 cm square or a half-round split from a larger section.
Green wood

ash shaves easily; seasoned ash is noticeably harder work under the drawknife. The grain must run along the full length of the stave with no knots in the working section. A knot in a hammer handle is a failure point. Coppice ash from a stool produces the most reliably straight-grained staves.
Tools
A
drawknife

on a shaving horse for the main shaping. A sloyd knife for refining the eye end and the grip chamfers. A leather strop. The shaving horse must hold the stave securely; test the grip before starting by pressing down on the horse treadle and trying to pull the blank free.
Method
Work the
blank

from square to oval section in stages. Avoid trying to reach the final diameter in one heavy pass; thin shavings give more control and a cleaner surface than removing large amounts of material quickly.

Mark the eye end profile on both faces of the blank. The eye end; the top 8 cm that fits into the hammer head; tapers from 3.5 cm at the top to 2.5 cm at the shoulder. Mark the shoulder line around all four faces.

Secure the stave in the shaving horse and work the main grip section to an oval 3 cm wide and 2.5 cm thick. Draw the drawknife toward you in long passes along the grain. Work with the grain; if the blade digs, reverse direction. Remove the corners first, then refine the oval.

Shape the eye end with the drawknife: reduce from 3.5 cm at the very top to 2.5 cm at the shoulder mark. The taper must be even on all four faces. Measure across both diameters with calipers or mark the target diameter on a scrap and hold the eye end next to it to compare.

Refine the grip end with the sloyd knife: a slight flare at the bottom (2 mm wider than the main grip) stops the hand sliding off under a heavy swing. Chamfer the bottom edge so it does not dig into the palm.

Leave the handle to season for 3 weeks before fitting. Test-fit the eye end into the hammer head before seasoning is complete; the handle should enter 3 cm and feel snug. As the wood dries it will shrink slightly; re-test and shave the eye end if needed before final fitting. Wedge the handle into the head with a hardwood wedge driven into a saw cut at the eye end.
Finishing

Leave the grip section unfinished; bare ash provides more friction than an oiled surface. Apply one coat of boiled linseed oil to the eye end and the top 10 cm to protect the wood where it is in contact with the metal head. One coat sets in 24 hours.
Variations
Hazel: works much like ash. Slightly shorter-lived under hard daily use.
Longer handle: 50 cm for a light axe or hatchet. The oval section becomes more pronounced; 3.5 cm wide, 2 cm thick; to give directional control during a swing.
Care
Re-oil the eye end annually. If the head loosens, soak the eye end in water overnight to swell the wood and re-tighten the joint. Store horizontally in a dry location.

attrs.intro: Common problems with the green ash tool handle.

items.fix: Soak the eye end in linseed oil for 12 hours, then re-drive the wedge. If the joint remains loose, remove the handle and shave a fresh taper on the eye end before re-fitting with a new wedge.
items.cause: Eye end fitted before the wood had finished drying
items.symptom: Handle loosens in the head after drying

items.fix: Flip the blank in the shaving horse and cut from the opposite end. Ash grain direction is usually easy to read; the shavings lift cleanly in the correct direction and tear in the wrong one.
items.symptom: Drawknife leaves a rough, torn surface

items.fix: The saw kerf should be centred across the eye end width and at least 4 cm deep. A short kerf causes the split to run past the intended depth. Cut the kerf before fitting the handle into the head.
items.cause: Saw kerf for the wedge not centred or too short
items.symptom: Eye end splits when the wedge is driven

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
